stories.title: Claude Code Hooks: Intercept and Control Every Step of the Agent Loop
stories.summary: Claude Code Hooks introduces a middleware layer that lets developers attach shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or custom LLM prompts to specific lifecycle events in the Claude Code agent loop — including UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, and PostToolUse. Hooks are triggered by pattern-matching on tool names or event types, and communicate back to the agent via JSON payloads and exit codes. Critically, hooks aren't passive observers: they can rewrite the prompt Claude sees next, block or defer specific tool calls before execution, or instruct Claude to retry a denied action. This gives teams programmatic control over agent behavior without forking the tool or relying on prompt engineering alone.
stories.considerations: The most immediate use case is enforcement in team environments: a PreToolUse hook on file write operations can run a linter or policy check and block the write if it fails, effectively making code style or security rules non-negotiable rather than advisory. Less obvious applications include audit logging pipelines (PostToolUse hooks that ship every tool call to a SIEM or compliance store), dynamic context injection (rewriting the prompt at UserPromptSubmit to prepend repo-specific conventions pulled from a live config service), or chaining a cheaper model as a pre-filter to reject low-confidence tool calls before they hit expensive operations. This matters now because as Claude Code gets used in higher-stakes or multi-developer contexts, the lack of guardrails has been the main blocker for adoption in regulated or production-adjacent workflows — hooks directly address that gap. The gotcha: hooks run synchronously in the agent loop, so a slow HTTP endpoint or a hung shell command will stall the entire agent; teams need to treat hook latency as a first-class concern and set aggressive timeouts from day one.

stories.title: Claude Code MCP: Connect Your Entire Toolchain to the AI
stories.summary: Claude Code now supports Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration, allowing developers to connect external tools — databases, issue trackers, monitoring services, APIs — directly into their Claude coding sessions. Instead of manually copying context from systems like Jira, Sentry, or PostgreSQL into the chat window, Claude can read from and write to those systems in real time during a session. MCP servers run in two modes: local (stdio-based subprocess) or remote (HTTP/SSE), and can also act as push channels — meaning Claude can reactively respond to incoming events like webhooks, Telegram messages, or Discord notifications. This turns Claude Code from a request-response coding assistant into a persistent ambient agent that can monitor and act on live infrastructure signals.
stories.considerations: The push-channel capability is the most underappreciated part here: a developer debugging a production incident could have Claude sit in a session subscribed to a Sentry webhook, automatically triaging new errors, cross-referencing the codebase, and drafting fixes as alerts fire — without the developer manually feeding each error into chat. Beyond the obvious DevOps use case, this opens doors for less obvious workflows: syncing Claude to a product analytics MCP so it can propose code changes in response to funnel drop-offs, or connecting a feature-flag service so Claude understands which code paths are actually live before suggesting refactors. This matters now because the biggest friction in AI-assisted coding isn't model quality — it's context assembly; developers spend significant time manually bridging the gap between their tools and their AI assistant, and MCP systematically eliminates that. The gotcha to flag: MCP server security is entirely on the developer — a misconfigured or compromised MCP server has read/write access to whatever systems you've connected, so treat MCP server provenance with the same scrutiny you'd give a CI/CD plugin; there's no sandboxing or permission scoping built into the protocol itself.

stories.title: Claude Code Sub-agents: Isolated Contexts That Preserve Your Main Conversation
stories.summary: Claude Code now exposes a formal sub-agent system that lets developers define specialized AI workers, each running in an isolated context window with its own system prompt, tool permissions, and model selection. When Claude detects a task matching a sub-agent's defined scope, it routes that work to the sub-agent automatically — keeping exploratory or bulk operations out of the primary conversation's context. The built-in Explore sub-agent already demonstrates the pattern: it runs on Claude Haiku with write tools explicitly denied, sandboxing codebase searches to a cheaper model without user intervention. Developers can now replicate this pattern for any repetitive side task — log parsing, test generation, file auditing — by defining custom sub-agents with scoped permissions and appropriate model tiers.
stories.considerations: The most immediate workflow impact is on large refactoring sessions: when you're restructuring a 50+ file monorepo, offloading grep-style exploration and read-heavy analysis to Haiku sub-agents means your primary Sonnet/Opus context stays clean and focused on actual decision-making, rather than getting bloated with file contents you've already processed. A non-obvious use case is compliance or security auditing — you could define a read-only sub-agent with no write or shell tools that scans for secrets, deprecated APIs, or license headers across a codebase, with zero risk of accidental mutation. This matters now because context window pollution is the silent killer of long agentic sessions; as developers push Claude Code into multi-hour autonomous workflows, a dirty context means degraded reasoning quality well before you hit token limits. The gotcha worth flagging: automatic task routing means you need to define sub-agent triggers carefully — overly broad matching could silently downgrade tasks to Haiku that actually need a more capable model, and since the routing happens without explicit confirmation, you may not notice until output quality drops.
